contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: For one sequential objective, avoid preprocessing that is not reused; for many static interval queries, compare full prefix build-plus-query cost, while updates may invalidate the static prefix representation.

items.prompt: An unsorted static array receives exactly one query for the sum of a known interval [l, r], and no later reuse is possible. Which baseline is usually sufficient?

options.text: Scan the requested interval once.
Always build an O(n)-space prefix array first.
Enumerate every possible window before answering.
Sort the interval before summing.

feedback.reason: One unrepeated query does not automatically justify a reusable index.
feedback.details: A direct scan costs O(r - l + 1) time and O(1) auxiliary space.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_mandatory: The one query may not repay preprocessing or memory.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_range: Sorting is unnecessary and changes order.

items.prompt: An immutable n-value array will receive q arbitrary range-sum queries. Which workload expression supports prefix sums?

options.text: O(n) preprocessing followed by O(1) arithmetic per query.
One moving window is O(1) regardless of how far query boundaries jump.
Sorting once makes original interval sums constant-time.
Every query is free because the array is static.

feedback.reason: Prefix state separates one build cost from repeated query cost.
feedback.details: The complete workload is O(n + q) time with O(n) retained prefix storage.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.move_between_queries: Moving between arbitrary ranges may cross many elements.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_free: Static data permits reuse but still requires an index.

items.prompt: The task asks for the maximum sum over every length-k block, visited from left to right exactly once. Which repeated-workload choice is strongest?

options.text: Use one rolling sum in O(n) time and O(1) auxiliary state.
A prefix array is the only valid way to reuse work.
Rescan k elements for every block.
Treat each block as an unrelated random query.

feedback.reason: Many intervals do not automatically imply prefix preprocessing.
feedback.details: Sequential adjacent queries can reuse a moving aggregate more compactly.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_only: Prefix sums are valid but use more state than this sequential workload needs.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_each: This repeats overlap and costs O(nk).

items.prompt: After each point update, later range sums must reflect the new value. What is the main issue with a plain static prefix array?

options.text: Changing a[i] invalidates every stored prefix after i, so rebuilding or another dynamic structure is required.
Prefix subtraction cannot handle positive values after an update.
Every dynamic range query becomes a sliding-window problem.
Sorting repairs all prefixes.

feedback.reason: Static prefix state encodes cumulative values of one array version.
feedback.details: Frequent updates can dominate if the whole suffix or array must be rebuilt.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.query_wrong_sign: The identity remains correct if prefixes are updated.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.window_required: Arbitrary dynamic queries may require a Fenwick tree, segment tree, or other structure.

items.prompt: A query workload asks for the sum of the most recent k stream values after every arrival. Why is a prefix array not automatically the best fit?

options.text: Only one frontier-local interval is needed, so a rolling sum can answer online with bounded state.
Prefix sums cannot represent stream prefixes.
The stream is immutable before it arrives.
A rolling window answers arbitrary historical ranges for free.

feedback.reason: Query count alone is not the decision boundary.
feedback.details: The access pattern is sequential and local, which favors moving state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_incorrect: They can, but retaining all of them may be unnecessary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.window_random: It represents only the current last-k interval.

items.prompt: Only two short interval queries will be asked on a very large array. Which conclusion is defensible?

options.text: Compare the total scanned lengths with O(n) prefix construction instead of assuming preprocessing wins.
Any q greater than one requires prefix sums.
A sliding window is always optimal for two queries.
Query volume never affects strategy.

feedback.reason: The relevant direct cost is the sum of queried interval lengths.
feedback.details: Prefix preprocessing is attractive only when its build cost is recovered or the representation serves another requirement.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_always: Two tiny scans may be much cheaper than reading all n elements for preprocessing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.window_always: Arbitrary ranges may not share a useful transition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_q: Reuse is central to preprocessing decisions.

items.prompt: A valid prefix array is persisted with the same immutable data version. A batch of new queries arrives. What should the cost model do?

options.text: Treat preprocessing as already paid and count only the new query work.
Charge another O(n) build for every batch by definition.
Use the prefix array even if the data version changed.
Discard the prefix array because windows are always linear.

feedback.reason: Workload analysis starts from the actual available state.
feedback.details: Version compatibility is the condition that makes reuse legal.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.switch_window: Existing reusable state changes the decision.

items.prompt: Future query count is unknown and may remain zero. Which strategy can avoid unnecessary preprocessing?

options.text: Answer directly at first and build prefix state only when observed cumulative query work justifies it.
Build every possible index immediately regardless of demand.
Unknown q makes a strategy impossible.
Sort after every query.

feedback.reason: A lazy policy can defer the O(n) build until there is evidence of reuse.
feedback.details: The transition must preserve one data version and should be based on cumulative work, not a keyword.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.eager_always: The work may never be used.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_later: Sorting is unrelated to original range sums.

items.prompt: Which workload facts belong in the one-query-versus-many decision? Select all that apply.

options.text: Number of queries.
Total or expected lengths of directly scanned intervals.
Update frequency and representation invalidation.
Only whether the prompt says contiguous.

feedback.reason: The decision is a lifecycle cost model.
feedback.details: Surface wording does not replace build, query, and update accounting.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.contiguous_word: Both methods operate on contiguous intervals.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.query_lengths: Direct cost may be far below qn.

items.prompt: Which workloads strongly favor static prefix preprocessing? Select all that apply.

options.text: Many arbitrary range-sum queries over unchanged data.
Repeated query batches reuse the same verified data version.
Several additive interval metrics are queried repeatedly.
Only the most recent k stream values are queried online.

feedback.reason: Prefix preprocessing is strongest when static cumulative state is reused broadly.
feedback.details: One frontier-local stream query does not consume the full index.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_last_k: A rolling window is more direct.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.many_random: Each query becomes a prefix difference.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.repeated_batches: The build is amortized across batches.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.multiple_metrics: Parallel prefix arrays can serve them.

items.prompt: Which workloads favor a moving or direct approach over a prefix array? Select all that apply.

options.text: One short interval query with no later reuse.
All fixed-size windows are visited sequentially once.
A last-k stream answer is required after each arrival.
Millions of random range queries over immutable data.

feedback.reason: Moving or direct state is strongest when preprocessing is not reused or candidates evolve locally.
feedback.details: Large random static workloads favor prefix state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.many_random_static: This is a strong prefix case.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.one_short_query: Scanning only the interval may be cheaper.

items.prompt: Which changes can invalidate or weaken prefix reuse? Select all that apply.

options.text: Point updates to the source array.
Loading a different data version.
Queries switch from sum to a non-additive property not represented by the prefixes.
The same range-sum workload receives more queries.

feedback.reason: A precomputed representation is useful only while its source and represented operation remain valid.
feedback.details: More compatible queries increase its value.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.version_change: Stored cumulative values no longer match.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.new_metric: The existing state may not answer it.

items.prompt: Arrange the workload comparison for direct scans versus prefix sums.

elements.text: Define n, query count q, interval lengths, updates, and required outputs.
Sum the direct work across all queried intervals.
Add prefix construction, compatible query work, and rebuilds.
Choose the legal path under time, space, and version-lifetime constraints.

feedback.reason: The comparison must count complete work on both paths.
feedback.details: Using q alone can hide short queries or update costs.

items.prompt: Arrange a static prefix-query lifecycle.

elements.text: Confirm the source data version is immutable for the representation's lifetime.
Answer each compatible interval query by subtraction.
Discard or rebuild when the source version changes.

feedback.reason: Prefix state is tied to one exact source version.
feedback.details: Reuse is safe only until a mutation or replacement invalidates it.

items.prompt: Arrange a lazy-preprocessing policy.

elements.text: Serve initial queries directly while tracking cumulative scan work.
Compare accumulated direct cost with prefix build and future expected demand.
Construct prefix state when the threshold is met and data is stable.
Route later compatible queries through the verified prefix representation.

feedback.reason: Lazy construction avoids paying for unused preprocessing.
feedback.details: The policy must prevent duplicate builds and reject stale data versions.

items.prompt: One range query spans m elements of an n-value array and no preprocessing exists. Select direct-scan time and auxiliary space.
constraints: The query answer is one scalar sum.

feedback.reason: The direct cost follows the queried interval length, not necessarily the full array size.
feedback.details: This is the correct baseline for a one-off short query.

items.prompt: q range queries have lengths m1 through mq and are answered by direct scans. Select total time and auxiliary space.
constraints: No query state is retained after its answer.

feedback.reason: Direct workload cost is the cumulative number of visited elements.
feedback.details: It equals O(qn) only in the worst case where every query spans Θ(n).

items.prompt: Build a prefix array for n static values and answer q scalar range-sum queries. Select total time and auxiliary space.
constraints: Each query uses two prefix reads and one subtraction.

feedback.reason: The build is linear and each compatible query is constant time.
feedback.details: The array remains live for the query workload.

items.prompt: Compute every length-k block sum in one sequential pass with a rolling sum. Select total time and auxiliary space.
constraints: The first block is summed once.
Each later block uses one subtraction and one addition.

feedback.reason: Sequential repeated intervals can be cheaper in memory than a reusable random-query index.
feedback.details: Each element enters and leaves the moving sum at most once.

items.prompt: Each of q queries scans the entire n-value array. Compare direct scans with one prefix build and q scalar range queries. Select both total times.

feedback.reason: Full-range rescanning repeats n work q times.
feedback.details: Prefix preprocessing wins asymptotically once the compatible query workload is reused.

items.prompt: The source array is replaced u times. For each version, a prefix array is rebuilt, and q scalar range queries are answered in total. Select total time and peak prefix space.
constraints: Only one version's prefix array is live at a time.

feedback.reason: Version churn multiplies preprocessing cost by u.
feedback.details: Peak space remains one n-sized prefix representation if old versions are released.

items.prompt: A direct approach answers q intervals whose total scanned length is L. A prefix approach builds n values and answers q queries. Select the two workload times.
constraints: Each direct visit and each prefix/query arithmetic operation costs O(1).

feedback.reason: The correct break-even comparison is L versus n + q.
feedback.details: This captures both many long queries and a few short queries without using a crude query-count rule.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: For one sequential objective, avoid preprocessing that is not reused; for many static interval queries, compare full prefix build-plus-query cost, while updates may invalidate the static prefix representation.
